title: Fine-Tuning for Classification
concept: We update both the classification head and the pretrained BERT weights on a small labeled dataset, quickly achieving high accuracy.

exercises.prompt: Why do we fine-tune a model like BERT?

options.text: To adapt a pretrained model to a specific task
To delete the model's memory completely
To make the model run slower

exercises.explain: Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained model to a specific task, like classification, using a small dataset.

cards.title: Meet Your Custom AI Classifier
cards.body: Pretrained models like BERT are smart, but they don't know what specific task you want them to do yet. Fine-tuning is the secret key that turns a general language giant into a specialized classification master in just minutes!

cards.title: What is Fine-Tuning?
cards.body: Fine-Tuning is the process of taking a pretrained model like BERT, adding a fresh classification layer on top, and training the whole network on a small, labeled dataset. This updates the model to fit your specific task.

cards.title: The Language Translator
cards.body: Imagine BERT is a professional translator who already knows general language. When you fine-tune BERT for customer reviews, it's like sending that translator to a short, specialized workshop on restaurant slang. They don't relearn language from scratch; they just adapt their existing skills to a new task!

cards.title: Spam or Not Spam?
cards.body: Suppose you want to classify emails as "Spam" or "Not Spam". You take a pretrained BERT model, add a classification head, and train it on 1,000 labeled emails. Because BERT already understands grammar, it learns to spot spam quickly.

cards.title: The Data Myth
cards.body: Many believe you need millions of examples to train BERT for your task. In reality, because BERT is already pre-trained, you only need a small labeled dataset (like a few hundred or thousand examples) to get high accuracy.

cards.title: Scratch vs. Fine-Tuned
cards.body: Here is how fine-tuning differs from using a raw, untrained model from scratch.

left.text: Starts with random weights. Needs massive data and time to learn simple words.

right.text: Starts with pretrained weights. Learns your specific task quickly with small data.

exercises.prompt: What do you need to fine-tune BERT for a specific classification task?

options.text: Nothing, it works instantly without training

exercises.explain: BERT already understands grammar and sentence structure, so it only needs a small labeled dataset to quickly adapt to your specific task.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Fine-tuning updates both the classification head and the pretrained BERT weights.
exercises.explain: Fine-tuning updates both the new classification head and the pretrained BERT weights to achieve the best performance.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence about how BERT adapts to a specific task.
exercises.sentence: Fine-tuning updates both the classification head and the pretrained BERT [blank] on a small dataset.
exercises.explain: During fine-tuning, the training process updates both the classification head and the core pretrained weights of the model.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes how fine-tuning updates a BERT model?

options.text: We add a classification head and update both its weights and the core BERT weights.
We delete the classification head and only train the original weights.
We keep all weights completely frozen and do not update anything.

exercises.explain: Option A is correct because fine-tuning updates both the classification head and the pretrained weights. Option B incorrectly claims it deletes the head, and Option C incorrectly claims nothing is updated.

exercises.prompt: When fine-tuning BERT for classification, what actually happens to the model's weights during training?

options.text: It deletes all of BERT's original layers and starts training from zero.
It updates both the classification head and the pretrained BERT weights.
It only changes the input text without updating any weights.
It locks the classification head and only trains the first layer.

exercises.explain: Fine-tuning updates both the classification head and the core BERT weights. This allows the model to adjust its deep understanding of language to fit your specific classification task perfectly.
